tldr: Headlines divide into two parallel currents: a majority of outlets — across Africa, the Arab world, Russia, and the UK — frame the story as Omar Artan's, foregrounding both the injustice of his US entry denial and his subsequent UEFA Super Cup appointment as redemption; a second stream, led by German, South American, and Australian outlets, casts the real story as Infantino's — a FIFA president deflecting with 'just chill' dismissals, defending his Trump-proximity, and labelled by Sueddeutsche Zeitung 'the man who thinks he is God.'

frames.sub_frame: US bars qualified referee from historic milestone

evidence.quote: Somali referee barred from entering the US for the World Cup
Somali referee denied US entry, dropped from FIFA World Cup
WM-Schiedsrichter aus Somalia trotz Visum abgewiesen
denied entry to U.S. sparks global uproar

frames.sub_frame: Artan as African hero; ban then UEFA redemption

evidence.quote: Artan picked for UEFA Super Cup after US World Cup ban
Top African Referee Omar Artan to Officiate Uefa Super Cup
Artan to referee Uefa Super Cup after losing World Cup role
barred by US given hero's welcome at home
Artan, banned by US, to officiate UEFA Super Cup
Infantino defends World Cup visa issues, ticket prices
FIFA boss defends World Cup tickets prices and brushes off
Infantino tries to sidestep World Cup controversies
FIFA-Boss Infantino verteidigt Ticketpreise und Trump-Nähe
The World Cup According to Gianni Infantino

frames.sub_frame: FIFA president as unchecked institutional autocrat

evidence.quote: „Der Mann, der sich für Gott hält“
The World Cup According to Gianni Infantino

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to lead with Artan's return to Somalia as a triumphant 'hero's welcome' homecoming rather than an injustice or loss — flips the narrative to vindication and advances an anti-US message without requiring direct criticism of US policy.

Only outlet to run a personality-portrait headline positioning Infantino as delusionally omnipotent ('the man who thinks he is God'), recasting the World Cup launch as an institutional legitimacy crisis rather than a logistical or diplomatic failure.
Only headline to characterise the story's global reception as a 'global uproar' — the strongest quantification of public reaction in the corpus, not matched by any other outlet.

bottom_line: The headline layer positions the 2026 World Cup launch as a dual crisis: an African referee denied a historic milestone by US immigration enforcement — redeemed by UEFA but not FIFA — and a FIFA president whose 'just chill' dismissals and Trump-proximity make his institution look as much like the problem as the US border.
